faq.a: Redshift (z) measures the shift of spectral lines toward longer wavelengths due to space expansion or recession. z = (λ_observed - λ_emitted) / λ_emitted.

faq.q: What's the difference between relativistic and non-relativistic redshift?
faq.a: For low z (z < 0.1), the simple formula v = z × c works well. For higher z, use: v = c × [(1+z)² - 1] / [(1+z)² + 1].

faq.q: What are the highest redshifts observed?
faq.a: The James Webb Space Telescope has detected galaxies with z = 10-13. Quasars can exceed z = 7, revealing the universe's earliest periods.

faq.q: How does this relate to Hubble's Law?
faq.a: Hubble's Law states v = H₀ × d, where H₀ ≈ 70 km/s/Mpc. It connects recession velocity to distance, explaining cosmic expansion.

faq.q: How is redshift measured in practice?
faq.a: Astronomers measure redshift by analyzing spectral lines (hydrogen-alpha, helium, etc.) shifted toward red wavelengths compared to laboratory values.
